notes: Standard insertion sort. Single input: vector<int> nums, length 1..1000, values in [-10^4, 10^4], duplicates allowed (not required distinct, not pre-sorted). No overflow concerns (values fit in int). Verified by compiling with clang++ -std=c++17 -O2 -w (replaced bits/stdc++.h with <vector>/<iostream> in the test harness since macOS clang lacks bits/stdc++.h; the class itself is header-agnostic). All examples reproduced: [7,4,1,5,3]->[1,3,4,5,7], [5,4,4,1,1]->[1,1,4,4,5]. Case 3 [3,2,3,4,5]->[2,3,3,4,5], matching the nowYourTurn correct option at index 2.

Classic single-source BFS shortest path on an undirected unweighted graph. Source is fixed at vertex 0; unit edge weights, so plain BFS gives shortest distances. Unreachable vertices return -1. Built adjacency list (undirected: add both directions), BFS from 0 with dist[]=-1 sentinel for unvisited. Inputs: edges = vector<vector<int>> of [u,v] pairs with u,v in [0,N-1]; N = number of vertices; M = number of edges. The signature passes N and M but M is just edges.size() and isn't strictly needed.

VERIFIED: compiled with clang++ -std=c++17 -O2 -w (using a local bits/stdc++.h shim since macOS clang lacks it) and reproduced all example/testcase outputs exactly: Example1 -> '0 1 2 1 2 3 3 4 4', Example2 -> '0 1 2 1 2 3 3 2', Case3 (n=3, edges=[[1,2]]) -> '0 -1 -1' (matches the 'now your turn' answer option index 2).

generatable=false: a valid input is a graph (edges array of vertex-pairs) with each entry constrained to [0, N-1] and consistent with N; the M/N relationship and vertex-index bounds cannot be expressed via the simple int-array/string knobs (sorted/distinct/domain). A generator must build a valid edge list (e.g. random pairs in [0,N-1], M = chosen edge count). value_lo/value_hi reflect the per-vertex index range only.

Classic monotonic-stack problem. For each element as subarray minimum, contribution = (i - prevStrictlySmaller) * (nextSmallerOrEqual - i) * arr[i], summed modulo 1e9+7. Asymmetric tie-breaking (strict on one side, non-strict on the other) handles duplicate values correctly. Constraints: 1 <= arr[i] <= 1e6, so value_lo=1, value_hi=1e6 (problem says positive integers; duplicates allowed, so distinct=false). max_n can be up to 1e5 per constraints but kept at 200 for fast oracle test generation; O(n) algorithm scales fine if larger. Generatable with a plain positive int array. Verified: compiled with clang++ -std=c++17 -O2 and reproduced all examples (18, 10) plus the 'Now Your Turn' case [11,81,94,43,3] -> 444.

Classic "Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock" (single transaction, at most once). Track running minimum price and maximize (price - min). Returns 0 when no profitable transaction exists. Method signature exactly matches starterCpp: int stockBuySell(vector<int> arr, int n). Random inputs: a single int array `arr` of length n with value range [0, 10^6] per constraints; pass n = arr.size(). No sorting/distinct/permutation constraints. No overflow concern (max profit < 10^6 fits in int). Verified by compiling with clang++ -std=c++17 -O2 -w (used explicit STL includes locally since macOS clang lacks bits/stdc++.h; the class itself needs no includes). All examples reproduced: [10,7,5,8,11,9]->6, [5,4,3,2,1]->0, and the "Now Your Turn" case [3,8,1,4,6,2]->5.

Heap + visited-set approach: sort both arrays ascending, push the largest pair (n-1,n-1) into a max-heap, pop k times, each pop expanding neighbors (i-1,j) and (i,j-1). A valid combination is nums1[i]+nums2[j] (ADDITION). Returns the k largest sums in non-increasing order. Verified: reproduces both example outputs ([13,9], [11,10]) and passed 20000 random brute-force cross-checks.

INPUT CONSTRAINTS the generator MUST enforce: (1) nums1 and nums2 must have EQUAL length n; (2) k must satisfy 1 <= k <= n*n. The k<=n*n bound is a dependency on n that the simple value_lo/value_hi/max_n knobs cannot express on their own, so the generator must compute k after choosing n. Elements are positive (1..10000 per constraints); value_lo/hi set accordingly. Sums fit in int (max 2*10^4) but long long is used internally for safety. No sorting/distinctness/permutation requirements on the arrays.

NOTE on the JSON's nowYourTurn: for nums1=[2,2], nums2=[5,5], k=2 every sum is 2+5=7, so the correct answer is [7,7] (option index 1). The JSON marks tcIndex=2 ([10,10]), which would require multiplication; that quiz metadata appears incorrect, but the testcase 'expected' for Case 3 is null so it does not affect oracle correctness. The oracle follows the problem statement (addition).

Classic "Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock III" - max profit with at most two transactions. O(n) state-machine DP with 4 states (buy1, sell1, buy2, sell2). Inputs are a single int array arr (0 <= arr[i] <= 1e6, 1 <= n <= 1e5). value_lo=0 since prices are non-negative per constraints; value_hi=1e6. Internal longs used defensively though int suffices (max profit < ~2e6). Verified: example outputs 15, 12 match; nowYourTurn case [5,7,2,10,6,9] yields 11 (option index 2). Note signature takes arr by value (copy), which is fine.

Input is a single n x n BINARY matrix (grid[i][j] in {0,1}), 2 <= n <= 5. The method signature takes only `grid` (no n); n is derived from grid.size(). Output is a vector<string> of all valid paths from (0,0) to (n-1,n-1), where each path uses moves D/L/R/U and no cell is revisited. The directions are explored in D, L, R, U order, which yields paths in lexicographically sorted order (matching the expected example output ["DDRDRR","DRDDRR"]). If grid[0][0]==0 or no path exists, returns an empty vector (the platform displays empty as "-1").

generatable=FALSE: a valid random input is a square 2D binary matrix, which cannot be expressed by the simple flat int-array / string generators (needs a 2D n x n square structure, not a 1D array). A custom generator producing an n x n grid of 0/1 values (recommended n in [2,5], and ideally grid[0][0]=grid[n-1][n-1]=1 to make non-trivial cases) would work. domain=binary and value_lo/hi=0/1 describe the per-cell value range. Verified: compiled with clang++ -std=c++17 -O2; reproduces example 1 (["DDRDRR","DRDDRR"]) and example 2 (empty -> -1) exactly, and example 3 grid yields a valid path.

Tarjan's articulation point algorithm. Input is an adjacency list (vector<int> adj[]) representing an undirected graph with n vertices (0..n-1); the graph may be disconnected and may contain self-loops. Self-loops/parent-skip are handled (parent check via the first matching neighbor; multi-edges to parent are safe enough for standard test data). Returns articulation points in ascending order, or {-1} if none. Verified by compiling with clang++ -std=c++17 (using a local stub for bits/stdc++.h since macOS clang lacks it) and reproducing all examples: Example1 -> [0, 2], Example2 -> [1, 4], triangle case (n=3, fully connected) -> [-1] (matches the JSON's null sentinel for 'no articulation points'). generatable=false: the input is a structured valid undirected graph (symmetric adjacency list with edges between valid vertex pairs), which cannot be expressed via simple random int arrays/strings; a graph generator is required. The JSON testcases provide edges in [[u,v],...] form plus n, so a harness must build the adjacency list from edge pairs.

Standard "DP on LIS" longest bitonic subsequence. left[i] = LIS ending at i (strict <), right[i] = longest strictly-decreasing subseq starting at i; answer = max(left[i]+right[i]-1). Uses STRICT inequalities, matching the GFG/Striver definition and both examples. The formula also permits purely increasing or purely decreasing sequences (no mandatory peak on both sides), which is required for Example 2 where the answer 8 is the whole monotone-then-monotone array and Example 1 gives 6. Single vector<int> input, no sortedness/distinctness/domain constraints. n <= 1000, values in [-1e6, 1e6]; max answer length ~1000 so int is safe (no overflow). O(n^2) time, trivially fast for n=1000. Verified by compiling (clang++ -std=c++17 -O2 with explicit headers since macOS lacks bits/stdc++.h; in target env bits/stdc++.h is assumed present) and reproducing both example outputs: 6 and 8.

Classic BFS flood fill. Critical edge case handled: when oldColor == newColor, return immediately to avoid infinite loop. Verified by compiling with clang++ -std=c++17 -O2 -w (replacing bits/stdc++.h with explicit includes for the local macOS test; the actual reference uses bits/stdc++.h as assumed present) and running all example testcases: Case1 -> [[2,2,2],[2,2,0],[2,0,1]], Case2 -> [[0,1,0],[1,1,0],[0,0,3]], Case3 (nowYourTurn) -> [[0,0,0],[0,0,0],[0,0,1]]. All match expected outputs/options.

generatable=false: inputs require a 2-D matrix (image) PLUS valid in-bounds seed coordinates (0<=sr<n, 0<=sc<m) and a newColor. These cannot be expressed as a simple flat int array/string; a custom generator must produce a 2D grid (1<=n,m<=50, values 0..65535) and pick sr in [0,n) and sc in [0,m). Coordinates depend on the generated matrix dimensions, so simple value-range/length params are insufficient.

Classic Longest Palindromic Substring via expand-around-center, O(n^2) time, O(1) extra space. Constraints: 1 <= s.length <= 1000, s consists of English letters (upper/lowercase) and digits. Input is a single string s.

IMPORTANT (tie-breaking): Multiple longest palindromes can exist (e.g. "babad" -> "bab" or "aba"). This reference returns the LEFTMOST longest palindrome (the first one found when scanning centers left-to-right and only updating on strictly greater length). This matches both examples ("babad"->"bab", "cbbd"->"bb"). When auto-generating tests, the oracle output must use THIS reference's exact choice; do not accept arbitrary equally-long answers unless the grader does palindrome+length checking. The displayed expected outputs wrap the string in double quotes (e.g. \"bab\"); the function itself returns the raw substring without quotes.

Generation: random string input. Use string domain with characters restricted to [a-zA-Z0-9]. max_n=1000. value_lo/value_hi are unused (string input) and set to 0. To exercise palindrome logic meaningfully, generators should bias toward a small alphabet (e.g. 2-4 distinct chars) so palindromes of nontrivial length appear; a fully random 62-char alphabet rarely produces palindromes longer than 1-2.

Verified: compiled with clang++ -std=c++17 -O2 -w (used explicit includes locally since macOS lacks bits/stdc++.h) and reproduced "babad"->"bab", "cbbd"->"bb"; "zabcdedcbaq"->"abcdedcba" (test case 3 expected was null and will be filled by the oracle; "abcdedcba" is the correct 9-char longest palindrome).

Single scalar int input d in [0,9]. Iterate positive integers from 1, accumulate the first 50 whose last digit (num % 10) equals d. For d=0 the first qualifying positive integer is 10, not 0, since integers must be > 0 — the loop naturally handles this because num starts at 1. Sums max out at 12750 (d=0), well within int range, but long long is used internally for safety. Verified: compiled with clang++ -std=c++17 -O2 -w (substituting <iostream> for bits/stdc++.h on macOS) and reproduced both example outputs (d=1 -> 12300, d=5 -> 12500) plus the 'now your turn' answer (d=2 -> 12350). Full output for d=0..9: 12750,12300,12350,12400,12450,12500,12550,12600,12650,12700. Generatable as a single int in 0..9 (set value_lo=0, value_hi=9, max_n=1).
